coredata.dc:title: A magnetic fluorescent spirochetes micromotor: Dynamic monitoring and in situ sterilization of foodborne pathogens
coredata.dc:creator: Sun, Feifei
Yao, Mingru
Su, Hang
Yang, Qingli
Wu, Wei
coredata.dc:description: Foodborne disease caused by foodborne pathogens is one of the most common, widespread and influential diseases in the world and poses a great threat to food safety and human health. It is worth noting that nondestructive detection and in situ sterilization of foodborne pathogens are of great significance for food nutritional retention. In this research, we constructed an aptamer fluorescent micromotor based on spirulina (SP) using aptamers as molecular recognition elements, nontoxic carbon quantum dots (CQDs) as fluorescent signal carriers, and polydopamine (PDA) as a thermal element to realize the accurate detection and in situ sterilization of pathogens in liquid food through the magnetic actuation of magnetized SP. It was proven that the detection limit of the aptamer fluorescent micromotor is 4 CFU/mL, the bactericidal rate can reach 100 %, and the detection recovery of the micromotor in milk samples is 92.89–106.96 %. In addition, the results showed that the protein retention rate of the micromotor after 30 s of near-infrared irradiation (NIR) was better than that of traditional pasteurization, which protected the food substrate as much as possible and provided great potential for practical application in food.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Aptamer fluorescent micromotor
Magnetic actuation
Foodborne pathogen
in situ sterilization
Near-infrared irradiation

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of magnetic fluorescent molecular probes
Construction of the aptamer fluorescent micromotor
Performance experiment of aptamer fluorescent micromotor
Detection and sterilization of L. monocytogenes
Principle of aptamer fluorescent micromotors for the detection and in situ sterilization of foodborne pathogens
Characterization of magnetic fluorescent molecular probes
Research on the performance of the aptamer fluorescent micromotor
Measurement of motion ability of the aptamer fluorescent micromotor
Cytotoxicity verification of the aptamer fluorescent micromotor
Detection and sterilization of L. monocytogenes
Detection of L. monocytogenes in real samples
Effect of sterilization on food substrate

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was financially supported by the National Key Research and Development Program of China [No. 2022YFF1102200 ], National Natural Science Foundation of China [No. U22A20541 ], the Breeding Plan of Shandong Provincial Qingchuang Research Team [No. 2019-135 ], the Qingdao Science and Technology Fund [No. 21-1-4-sf-6-nsh ], High-value Intensive Processing and Development of Multi-level Coproduction Processing System of Rosa roxburghii [No. 6602421190 ] and the High-level Talent Start-up Fund from Qingdao Agricultural University [No. 663/1121045 ]. We thank Haier Biomedical for NIR technology assistant, and thank Dr. Jie Liu for magnetic drive motion assistant.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Construction of aptamer fluorescent micromotors and its application in off-on fluorescence detection and in situ sterilization of pathogens.

Characterization of CQDs. (A) The preparation process of CQDs. (B) Fluorescence emission spectra collected from CQDs420. (C) Photos of CQDs solution in daylight and under a 365 nm lamp. (D) TEM image of CQDs.

Characterization of PDA. (A) Photos of color changes during PDA synthesis in daylight. (B) TEM images changes during PDA synthesis. (C) Polymerization degree of PDA at different polymerization times. (D) Ultraviolet spectra during PDA synthesis. (E) Fourier infrared spectrum of PDA.

Characterization and optimization of magnetic fluorescent molecular probes. (A) The preparation process of the magnetic fluorescent molecular probe. (B) 3 % agarose gel electrophoresis result for A1 @A2: lane 1, DNA marker; lane 2, A2; lane 3, A1; and lane 4, A1 @A2. (C) TEM images during MBs@PDA synthesis and comparison images of MBs and MBs@PDA. (D) 3 % agarose gel electrophoresis result for magnetic molecular probe: lane 1, DNA marker; lane 2, 1 μmol/L A1 @A2; lane 3, 4, 5, 6, the supernatant of 200 μL of 1 μmol/L A1 @A2 connected with 50 μg, 100 μg, 150 μg, 200 μg of MBs@PDA, respectively. (E) Fluorescent emission spectra of optimization of magnetic fluorescent molecular probes.

Characterization of aptamer fluorescent micromotors. (A) The synthesis process of the aptamer fluorescent micromotor. (B) Diagram of potential changes in SP and the magnetic fluorescent molecular probe under different pH values. (C) Photos of the state of SP at different pH values. (D) The element mapping diagrams of the scanning electron microscopic image of the aptamer fluorescent micromotor (including P in SP, Fe in MBs, and C, O, N in CQDs).

Cytotoxicity verification of the aptamer fluorescent micromotor. (A) Histogram of cell growth And (B) Cell growth curves (the cells were treated with ultrapure water, SP, the magnetic fluorescent molecular probe and the aptamer fluorescent micromotor within four days).

Measurement of the photothermal effect of the aptamer fluorescent micromotor. (A) Thermal images of temperature changes in different amounts of aptamer fluorescent micromotors under different NIR times. (B) Broken line diagrams of temperature changes in different amounts of aptamer fluorescent micromotors under different NIR times.

Detection and sterilization effect test. (A) The process for detection and sterilization effect testing of aptamer fluorescent micromotors. (B) Calibration curve for L. monocytogenes detection with blank control and 1–105 CFU/mL, the linear regression equation was y = 54.414ln(x)+ 10.02, with R 2 = 0.9848. (C) The fluorescence intensity when detecting different bacteria (Y. enteritidis, Salmonella, E. coli O157:H7, L. monocytogenes) at the same concentration of 106 CFU/mL and blank control. (D) Plate comparison diagrams of sterilization effect with different treatment methods for different treatment times. (E) Flow cytometry of the sterilization effect of aptamer fluorescent micromotor-NIR.
CD spectra of different treatment methods for milk samples.
Oligonucleotide sequences used in this study.

head.ce:title: A magnetic fluorescent spirochetes micromotor: Dynamic monitoring and in situ sterilization of foodborne pathogens

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Food Science and Engineering, Qingdao Agricultural University, Qingdao 266109, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Food Science and Engineering, Qingdao Agricultural University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Food Science and Engineering, Qingdao Agricultural University, Qingdao 266109, China;

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Qingdao Special Food Research Institute, Qingdao 266109, China

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Laboratory of Quality & Safety Risk Assessment for Fruit (Qingdao), Ministry of Agriculture and Rural Affairs, Qingdao 266109, China

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Laboratory of Quality & Safety Risk Assessment for Fruit (Qingdao), Ministry of Agriculture and Rural Affairs, Qingdao 266109, China.

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: College of Food Science and Engineering, Qingdao Agricultural University, Qingdao 266109, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Food Science and Engineering, Qingdao Agricultural University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Foodborne disease caused by foodborne pathogens is one of the most common, widespread and influential diseases in the world and poses a great threat to food safety and human health. It is worth noting that nondestructive detection and in situ sterilization of foodborne pathogens are of great significance for food nutritional retention. In this research, we constructed an aptamer fluorescent micromotor based on spirulina (SP) using aptamers as molecular recognition elements, nontoxic carbon quantum dots (CQDs) as fluorescent signal carriers, and polydopamine (PDA) as a thermal element to realize the accurate detection and in situ sterilization of pathogens in liquid food through the magnetic actuation of magnetized SP. It was proven that the detection limit of the aptamer fluorescent micromotor is 4 CFU/mL, the bactericidal rate can reach 100 %, and the detection recovery of the micromotor in milk samples is 92.89–106.96 %. In addition, the results showed that the protein retention rate of the micromotor after 30 s of near-infrared irradiation (NIR) was better than that of traditional pasteurization, which protected the food substrate as much as possible and provided great potential for practical application in food.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The data that support the findings of this study are available in the Supporting Information of this article.

ce:section.ce:para: Foodborne pathogens are a serious threat to global food safety. Due to their pathogenicity, these pathogens can cause serious illness and even death in humans [1,2]. Therefore, people are increasingly focused on developing an effective strategy for rapid detection and sterilization of pathogens [3]. However, most of the developed detection strategies generally adopt a sampling method for spot checks, and the detection results are not comprehensive [4,5]. Common methods of killing pathogens are usually achieved by heating the whole food product, which is not a targeted process [6–8]. The existing methods have difficulty simultaneously detecting and killing pathogens in situ; therefore, it is of great significance to construct a universal platform for the nondestructive detection and in situ sterilization.

The in situ sterilization method based on PDA nanomaterials is considered to be a promising new method [9]. PDA is a natural biopolymer with good stability and good biodegradability [10]. From the perspective of material application, PDA has three primary advantages: first, the strong NIR absorption characteristics of PDA enable it to produce an excellent photothermal effect [11]; second, PDA has different surface reactivity and can adhere to the solid surface to form a dense composite layer film [12,13]; third, PDA has high fluorescence quenching, and off-on fluorescence detection can be realized with the help of additional fluorescent probes [13]. The combination of these characteristics makes it possible for PDA to in situ sterilization of pathogens. Xie et al. [14] introduced a PDA coating on magnetic microswimmers for photothermal treatment of Klebsiella pneumoniae in vivo, which shows the great potential of PDA in practical applications.

However, the prerequisite for in situ sterilization is to achieve in situ detection of pathogens. In recent years, micro/nanobots have attracted widespread attention [15–19]. To date, many micro/nanobots have been used in medical diagnosis, in vivo imaging, detection of pathogens and other fields [14,20]. The greatest advantage of aptamer fluorescent micromotors is that they can realize dynamic detection and targeted in situ sterilization in actual liquid samples. Compared with common biosensors, micromotors with spirochetes use degradable SP through magnetization to drive magnetically in various fluid environments [16,21–26]. Yan and Xie et al. [14,22,27] obtained multifunctional spiral microrobots by magnetizing SP, which can realize real-time tracking and monitoring of pathogens through high precision and depth control navigation. Commonly used heat treatment methods, such as pasteurization, are of great significance in ensuring the microbial quality of food. However, these methods can easily change the physical and chemical structure of nutrients and damage the food matrix [6]. In situ sterilization of aptamer fluorescent micromotors based on the PDA photothermal effect is more beneficial to protect food matrices. As a common biological recognition element, antibodies are expensive, require low-temperature storage and have gradually shown detection limitations [28]. However, aptamers have become the key to capturing pathogens due to their advantages of low production cost, easy chemical modification, high binding efficiency, and easy in vitro synthesis [29,30]. Biosensors based on colorimetric and electrochemical methods have been widely constructed for pathogens detection, and their timeliness and reaction instability limit their practical application [5,31]. In contrast, stable fluorescence detection has higher practicability. CQDs, as fluorescent semiconductor nanocrystals [32], are widely used as fluorescent probes due to their good photoluminescence properties and good stability. CQDs can track pathogens for a long time and obtain quantitative information to achieve rapid and sensitive detection [33–35].

Herein, we constructed an efficient and sensitive magnetic aptamer fluorescent micromotor for rapid, nontoxic and sensitive fluorescence detection and in situ sterilization of pathogens through the deposition and enrichment of functional substances such as aptamers, CQDs and PDA. The nucleic acid sensing sterilization method used in this research are simple and easy to operate, have a low cost of detection and sterilization, and provide notable fluorescence signals, which is of great significance to improve food quality and control the pollution of foodborne pathogens.

Citric acid, urea, NaCl and MgCl2 were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). The 1000 d dialysis bag, dopamine hydrochloride (DA-HCl), streptavidin (SA) and 1x PBS buffer (0.01 M, pH 7.2–7.4) were supplied by Solarbio Technology Co., Ltd. (Beijing, China). Tris-HCl (1 M, pH 8.0), agarose powder, GelRed stain, 50x TAE buffer, 6x glycerin gel buffer, phosphate-buffered saline (PBS), and DNA molecular weight standard Marker A (25–500 bp) were all obtained from Sangon Biotech (Shanghai, China). EDC was purchased from SigmaAldrich (Shanghai, China). NHS was obtained from McLean (Shanghai, China). Magnetic beads (MBs) (100 nm) were purchased from Tianjin Beisile Chromatographic Technology Development Center (Tianjin, China). Goldview stain was supplied by Baihao Biotechnology Co., Ltd. (Beijing, China). SP was purchased from Taobao. Yersinia enterocolitica, (Y. enteritidis), Salmonella typhimurium (Salmonella), Listeria monocytogenes (L. monocytogenes), and Escherichia coli (E. coli) O157:H7 were all obtained from Guangdong Microbial Culture Preservation Center (Guangdong, China). Propidium iodide (PI) was purchased from Thermo Fisher (USA). The water used throughout the experiment was ultrapure water (18.25 MΩ/cm, 25 °C). Two aptamers were synthesized by Sangon Biotechnology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). The oligonucleotide sequences of L. monocytogene aptamer 1 (A1) and strand A complementary to the A1 part (A2) are shown in Table 1. The oligonucleotide sequences used in this study were taken from previously reported literature [36].

Fluorescence detection was performed using an F-2700 Fluorescence Spectrophotometer (Hitachi Ltd., Japan). The topography was evaluated by a JEM-2100 F transmission electron microscopy (TEM) instrument (Hitachi Ltd., Japan). Gel electrophoresis experiments were performed using a Mini-Sub Cell GT System (Bio-Rad Laboratories, Lnc. USA.). All UVvis spectra were obtained using an Evolution 201 UVVis spectrophotometer (Thermo Fisher, USA). Microscopic images were obtained by a BX53 +DP80 fluorescence microscope (OLYMPUS, Japan). Group analysis was obtained by Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (Thermo Scientific, USA). The PI staining experiment was conducted by a FACS AriaIII Flow cytometry (FC) instrument (BD, USA). Secondary structure of the protein were scanned by circular dichroism (CD) spectroscopy (Applied Photophysics Ltd., UK). The zeta potential was measured by a Malvern dynamic scatterometer (Malvern Ltd., UK).

CQDs were prepared by a one-step hydrothermal method with citric acid and urea as raw materials. Specifically, citric acid and urea were mixed and dissolved in 20 mL of ultrapure water and then transferred to a high-pressure reactor at 180 °C for 6 h, and then the uncarbonized part was removed by centrifugation. Finally, CQDs with uniform particle sizes were obtained by dialysis.

PDA was synthesized from DA by self-polymerization oxidation. Specifically, DA-HCl powder (7.5 mg) was mixed in 30 mL of Tris-HCl buffer (pH 8.0). The mixture was placed in a thermostatic oscillator and shaken gently and continuously for 3 h at room temperature, and PDA was gradually formed.

The lyophilized powders of A1 and A2 were diluted to the working concentration (10 μmol/L) with ultrapure water, the same amount of A1 and A2 were mixed, and the paired salt solution (100 mmol/L NaCl, 5 mmol/L MgCl2, and 10 mmol/L Tris-HCl) was added according to the volume ratio of 10:1. After mixing, A1 @A2 was synthesized by PCR treatment (95 °C for 10 min, 30 °C for 30 min, 4 °C for 20 min).

MBs (100 nm, 10 mg) were mixed with 10 mL of the above prepared PDA solution, and MBs@PDA were synthesized by adhesion of PDA after shaking gently at room temperature for 3 h. Then, the MBs@PDA were magnetically separated, washed three times and stored for later use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of magnetic fluorescent molecular probes
ce:section.ce:para: A1 @A2 was diluted to 2 μmol/L, and an appropriate amount of EDC and NHS were added for the activation of A1-5′-COOH for 30 min, then the A1 @A2 was mixed with 800 μg MBs@PDA, and oscillated at room temperature for 1 h. The magnetic molecular probes were synthesized by amidation between A1-5′-COOH and PDA-NH2.

A total of 100 μL of CQD solution was mixed with 200 μL of 1 mg/mL SA solution, and an appropriate amount of EDC and NHS were added. Then, the mixed liquid was shaken at 37 °C for 3 h and overnight at 4 °C to synthesize CQDs-SA.

The synthesized CQD-SA solution was mixed with the synthesized magnetic molecular probes according to a volume of 1:1. The magnetic fluorescent molecular probes were synthesized by the connection of CQDs-SA and A2–5′-biotin after shaking at room temperature for 1 h. Finally, the probe was magnetically separated, washed three times and resuspended in ultrapure water.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Construction of the aptamer fluorescent micromotor
ce:section.ce:para: The pH values of SP and magnetic fluorescent molecular probes were adjusted to 3, 4, 5, 6, 7, 8, 9, 10, 11, and 12, and the potentials at different pH values were measured. When the potentials of SP and the magnetic molecular fluorescent probe were opposite to each other, the two were mixed and gently oscillated at room temperature for 24 h. The magnetic fluorescent molecular probe was continuously attached to the surface of SP, and aptamer fluorescent micromotors were formed.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Performance experiment of aptamer fluorescent micromotor

ce:section.ce:para: The CCK-8 method was used to verify the cytotoxicity of the aptamer fluorescent micromotor. Ultrapure water was used as a control, and the resuscitated cells were treated with SP, magnetic fluorescent molecular probe and aptamer fluorescent micromotor and cultured in a 96-well plate. The cell concentration was adjusted to 1 × 104 CFU/mL, 100 μL per well. Cells at each time point (0 h, 24 h, 48 h, 72 h) were collected and added to CCK-8 solution (ca. KGA317) at a ratio of 1/10. After 4 h of incubation, the plate was read by the microplate reader, and OD450 data were read by CCK-8.

The photothermal effect was measured at room temperature and in the atmosphere. Radiation was generated by NIR with an excitation wavelength of 808 nm, a power of 2 W, and an energy density of 2.5 mJ/cm2. Heat images were obtained by an infrared thermal imaging camera. The temperature was read directly from the thermal image by Shendawei software. The prepared samples (0 μg, 10 μg, 20 μg, 30 μg, 40 μg, 50 μg) were added to the glass plate and irradiated by NIR for 0, 10, 20, 30, 40, 50, 60 s to compare the photothermal effects.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection and sterilization of L. monocytogenes

ce:section.ce:para: Y. enteritidis, Salmonella, L. monocytogenes, and E. coli O157:H7 were cultured in LB liquid medium at 37 °C for 8 h. The cultured bacterial solution was centrifuged and washed 3 times with PBS buffer and resuspended in PBS buffer. The concentration of pathogens was estimated by the absorbance value at 600 nm.

The PBS bacterial suspension was mixed with the aptamer fluorescent micromotors and oscillated slowly at room temperature. L. monocytogenes were caught by aptamer fluorescent micromotors, and A2-CQDs were released into the system. The specificity and sensitivity were verified by measuring the fluorescence intensity of the supernatant.

PBS bacterial suspension (200 μL) at a concentration of 106 CFU/mL was mixed with 100 μg of aptamer fluorescent micromotors and added to the 1 mL sample, and slowly oscillated at room temperature. The pathogens were captured by aptamer fluorescent micromotors and treated with NIR for 0, 15, 30, 45 and 60 s. Then, the bacterial suspension with the same concentration was treated by PBS-NIR and 72 °C pasteurization, and the same time gradient was applied to the control. The bactericidal effect was verified by plate coating method and flow cytometry analysis. This method was conducted in accordance to a previously published method [37].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Principle of aptamer fluorescent micromotors for the detection and in situ sterilization of foodborne pathogens
ce:section.ce:para: In this study, a magnetic aptamer fluorescent micromotor was designed for off-on fluorescence detection and photothermal sterilization of pathogens. As shown in Fig. 1, firstly, we constructed a magnetic fluorescent molecular probe, then attached it to SP to synthesize the aptamer fluorescent micromotor. The SP realized the spiral motion of the micromotor in the sample to dynamically capture pathogens, and the magnetic fluorescent molecular probe realized the amplification of the fluorescence signal and the enhancement of the photothermal effect. CQDs near PDA will produce fluorescence quenching. The affinity of pathogens and A1 is stronger than that of A2, so when A2-CQDs are competed by pathogens, fluorescence recovery will occur because CQDs are far away from PDA. Therefore, the detection of pathogens can be realized by off-on fluorescence. After NIR, MBs@PDA heated rapidly, and in situ sterilization of pathogens could be realized in a short time. This method realizes targeted sterilization while rapid detection, which is of great significance in practical application.

The preparation process of CQDs is shown in Fig. 2A. To explore the properties of CQDs, a fluorescence spectrophotometer and TEM were used for characterization. At different excitation wavelengths, the CQDs had a common fluorescence absorption peak at 420 nm, and the highest absorption peak at an excitation wavelength of 320 nm (Fig. 2B). The colors under visible light (left) and ultraviolet (right) intuitively showed the blue fluorescence characteristics of CQDs (Fig. 2C), and the fluorescence intensity decreased with decreasing concentration. The TEM image showed that the CQDs were evenly dispersed, with a size of 3–5 nm (Fig. 2D). In addition, using quinine sulfate as a fluorescent standard material, the quantum yield of CQDs was calculated to be 25.04 % (Fig. S1 A and B).

PDA was formed by the self-polymerization and oxidation of DA. To verify the formation of PDA and explore the properties of PDA, a UVVis spectrophotometer, TEM and Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy were used for characterization. Fig. 3A shows the color change under visible light during the formation of PDA for 0–3 h, which gradually changed from colorless to brownyellow. Fig. 3B shows the TEM image during the formation of PDA for 0–3 h. With increasing duration, PDA was gradually formed by the aggregation of DA. After 3 h of polymerization, PDA showed a spot-like aggregation morphology with a size of approximately 50 nm. Fig. 3C showed the degree of polymerization at different times. In addition, PDA has an absorption peak at 282 nm in the ultraviolet spectrum (Fig. 3D). The reason for the wide absorption peak is that the size and shape of PDA vary with the degree of DA accumulation. Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy was used to analyze the surface functional groups of PDA (Fig. 3E), the peaks of PDA at 700 cm−1, 1640–1600 cm−1 and 3480–3270 cm−1 correspond to the in-plane bending vibration of NH2, the variable angle vibration of NH2 and the telescopic vibration of NH2, respectively [38], which proved that PDA contains many NH2 groups.

The synthesis of A1 @A2 was verified by 3 % agarose electrophoresis (0.01 % Gel Red stain) ( Fig. 4B). Lanes 1, 2, 3 and 4 were 10 μL of 25–500 bp Marker, 10 μL of 2 μmol/L A2, 10 μL of 2 μmol/L A1, and 10 μL of 2 μmol/L synthesized A1 @A2, respectively. The gel imaging showed that the electrophoretic migration of A2 in lane 2 was the fastest, and the molecular weight of A2 was the smallest, approximately 20 bp. The migration of A1 in lane 3 was slightly lagging behind because the molecular weight of A1 was slightly larger than that of A2, approximately 54 bp, and the migration of A1 @A2 in lane 4 was the slowest because A1 and A2 were partially complementary and paired, resulting in a larger molecular weight of A1 @A2, which was approximately 74 bp. Therefore, the experiment showed that A1 @A2 was successfully synthesized.

According to the adhesion of PDA to solid substances, PDA can naturally accumulate on the surface of MBs to form a layer of PDA coating. The TEM images (Fig. 4 C) showed the changes in the MB surface after deposition of PDA for 1–3 h. With increasing duration, the PDA coating became thicker. And the diameter of MBs@PDA significantly increased by approximately 50 nm compared with MBs. In addition, the UV spectrum of the supernatant after PDA adhered to MBs was significantly lower than that of the PDA solution (Fig. S2), indicating that the concentration of PDA decreased, which proved the successful synthesis of MBs@PDA.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of magnetic fluorescent molecular probes
ce:section.ce:para: The preparation process of the magnetic fluorescent molecular probe is shown in Fig. 4 A. And it was verified by 3 % agarose electrophoresis (0.01 % Gold View stain) of the reaction supernatant. As shown in Fig. 4D, lane 1 was 10 μL of 25–500 bp Marker; lane 2 was 10 μL of 1 μmol/L A1 @A2; and lanes 3, 4, 5, 6 were the 10 μL supernatant of 200 μL, 1 μmol/L A1 @A2 connected with 50 μg, 100 μg, 150 μg, and 200 μg of MBs@PDA, respectively. The gel imaging showed that the electrophoretic band of lane 2 was the brightest, indicating that the concentration of A1 @A2 was the highest. With the increasing amount of MBs@PDA added, the electrophoretic bands became shallower and shallower, indicating that the concentration of A1 @A2 gradually decreased, which proved that A1 @A2 was successfully connected with MBs@PDA to synthesize magnetic molecular probes. Because the electrophoretic band of lane 5 was almost insignificant, it was determined that 200 μL of 1 μmol/L A1 @A2 and 150 μg of MBs@PDA were the optimal amounts.

The synthesis of magnetic fluorescent molecular probes was determined by a fluorescence spectrophotometer. Fig. 4E showed the fluorescence of the supernatant decreased gradually with increasing magnetic molecular probes, indicating that the concentration of CQDs-SA in the supernatant decreased gradually, which proved the successful combination of CQDs-SA and magnetic molecular probes, and magnetic fluorescent molecular probes were formed. When 350 μg of magnetic molecular probe was added, the fluorescence of the supernatant no longer decreased, which proved that 350 μg of magnetic molecular probe and 100 μL of CQDs-SA were the best combination amounts.

The synthesis process of the aptamer fluorescent micromotor is shown in Fig. 5A. Fig. 5B showed the potentials of SP and the magnetic fluorescent molecular probe at different pH values. When pH< 7, the magnetic fluorescent molecular probe showed positive potential, while SP showed negative potential. The potentials of the two were opposite, and the magnetic fluorescent molecule probe can be attached to the SP surface by electrostatic adsorption. However, Fig. 5C and Fig. S3 showed the condition of excess acidity eliminated the SP content, seriously affecting the state of SP, so pH 6–7 is the best condition.

The synthesis of the aptamer fluorescent micromotor was characterized by fluorescence microscopy and scanning electron microscopy element mapping. From the contrast of Fig. S4A and B, magnetic fluorescent molecular probes were uniformly attached to the surface of SP. The element mapping diagram of the scanning electron microscopic image (Fig. 5D) showed the distribution of P in SP, Fe in MBs and C, O, N in CQDs, which proved the synthesis of the aptamer fluorescent micromotors.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Research on the performance of the aptamer fluorescent micromotor
Measurement of motion ability of the aptamer fluorescent micromotor
ce:section.ce:para: We simulated the magnetic drive motion of the aptamer fluorescent micromotor in milk samples. As shown in video S1, we simulated the magnetic driving motion of a single aptamer fluorescent micromotor, and the micromotor spiraled downward at a uniform speed. As shown in video S2, we simulated the magnetic drive motion of multiple aptamer fluorescent micromotors, and all of the micromotors spiraled to the left under the magnetic drive. As shown in video S3, the motion track of a single aptamer fluorescent micromotor is shown by the red line. Under the action of a magnetic drive, the aptamer fluorescent micromotor can control the change in motion direction and accurately control navigation.
Supplementary material related to this article can be found online at doi:10.1016/j.snb.2023.133679.

The following is the Supplementary material related to this article Video S1, Video S2, Video S3.
Video S1
Video S2
Video S3
.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cytotoxicity verification of the aptamer fluorescent micromotor
ce:section.ce:para: The CCK-8 method was used to verify the cytotoxicity of the aptamer fluorescent micromotor. Fig. 6A and B showed that the OD450 data and growth curve of cells treated with SP, magnetic fluorescent molecular probe and aptamer fluorescent micromotor within four days had little difference from those of the control and ultrapure water treatment, indicating that the three did not affect the growth and reproduction of cells, which proved that the aptamer fluorescent micromotor synthesized in this experiment had no toxicity.

The introduction of a PDA coating can enhance the photothermal effect. The purpose of this section is to verify the relationship between the photothermal effect and experimental parameters (sample quantity of aptamer fluorescent micromotor and NIR time). Fig. 7A and B showed the temperature continuously went through two stages (a steady rise and a steady trend) with increasing irradiation time. The temperature after stabilization was positively correlated with the sample quantity. The temperature of the 40 μg micromotors illuminated by NIR for 30 s reached 65 °C (the threshold required for photothermal therapy). In contrast, the temperature of the blank was much lower for the same measurement. These results confirmed that the PDA coating can enhance the photothermal effect.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection and sterilization of L. monocytogenes
ce:section.ce:para: The flow chart of the aptamer fluorescent micromotor used to detect and kill pathogens is shown in Fig. 8 A.

We compared the capture efficiency of the aptamer fluorescent micromotor and the magnetic fluorescence molecular probe by optimizing the incubation time. As shown in the Fig. S5A and B, the optimal incubation time of the magnetic fluorescence molecular probe is 60 min, and the optimal incubation time of the micromotor is 30 min. When the incubation time is 30 min, the capture efficiency of the micromotor is significantly higher than that of the magnetic fluorescence molecular probe, so it is proved that the micromotor improves the capture efficiency.

The sensitivity of the developed micromotor for pathogens detected was determined, and L. monocytogenes was used as an example. In this step, different concentrations of L. monocytogenes were detected (Fig. 8B), with increasing concentrations of L. monocytogenes, the fluorescence intensity of the supernatant also gradually increased. The regression equation of L. monocytogenes was y = 54.414ln(x)+ 10.02, with R 2 = 0.9848, and the detection limit was 4 CFU/mL.

The specificity of the developed micromotor for pathogens detected was determined, and L. monocytogenes was taken as an example. In this step, the constructed micromotor was used to detect Y. enteritidis, Salmonella, E. coli O157:H7 and L. monocytogenes at the same bacterial concentration, and PBS was used as a blank control. Fig. 8 C shows that the aptamer fluorescent micromotor responds to L. monocytogenes with a strong fluorescence signal, so the micromotors had high specificity.

The bactericidal effect of the aptamer fluorescent micromotor irradiated by NIR was verified by the plate coating method and flow cytometry. PBS bacterial suspensions with the same concentration and volume were treated by aptamer fluorescent micromotor-NIR, PBS-NIR and 72 °C pasteurization for 0 s, 15 s, 30 s, 45 s, and 60 s, respectively. Fig. 8D showed the bactericidal effect of the bacterial suspension treated with aptamer fluorescent micromotor-NIR improved with increasing NIR time. However, the bactericidal effect of the bacterial suspension treated with PBS-NIR was not obvious with increasing NIR time. The bactericidal effect of the bacterial suspension treated by 72 °C pasteurization was obvious only when the treatment time was 60 s. Compared with PBS-NIR and 72 °C pasteurization, the aptamer fluorescent micromotor-NIR had a better bactericidal effect in a shorter time. In addition, we verified the bactericidal effect of the aptamer fluorescent micromotor subjected to NIR for 0 and 30 s by FC (Fig. 8E), when the treatment time was 0 s, the bacterial community concentrated in the Q4 area, and after 30 s of treatment, the dead bacteria gradually moved to the Q1 area by PI dye staining. It was proven that the bactericidal effect of aptamer fluorescent micromotor-NIR was significant.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of L. monocytogenes in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: Milk was selected as the test sample to evaluate the practical feasibility of the scheme through recycling experiments. The milk was pretreated with a 100-fold dilution. L. monocytogenes with different concentrations of 102, 103, and 104 CFU/mL were added to blank milk samples to verify the detection effect of aptamer fluorescent micromotors in real samples. As shown in Table S1, the recovery rate of L. monocytogenes in milk samples was 92.89–106.96 %.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of sterilization on food substrate
ce:section.ce:para: Milk is an essential source of protein for humans, it has good antioxidant activity, immune-enhancing properties, and the ability to prevent gastrointestinal infection [39–41]. Commonly, milk requires heat treatment to improve the microbial quality. However, conventional pasteurization and other heat treatment methods have a great impact on the nutritional value of protein in milk [42], and the aptamer fluorescent micromotor proposed in this research solved this problem. The effect of the aptamer fluorescent micromotor-NIR on the protein structure in milk was determined by a PiStar kinetic CD spectrometer (Table S2 and Fig. 9), the α helix structure and β folding of milk protein treated with aptamer fluorescent micromotor-NIR decreased and increased much slighter than 72 °C pasteurization, which proved the effect of the aptamer fluorescent micromotor-NIR on the food substrate is significantly less than that of 72 °C pasteurization.

In conclusion, an aptamer fluorescent micromotor was constructed for dynamic detection and targeted in situ sterilization of foodborne pathogens. The detection limit of the micromotor is 4 CFU/mL, the recovery rate is 92.89–106.96 %, and the micromotor has good sensitivity and specificity. At the same time, the micromotor realizes in situ sterilization of pathogens through a small part heating area under the action of near infrared, as far as possible to protect the food substrate, which is better than pasteurization. In this method, the aptamer used reduced the detection cost, the CQDs used improved the fluorescence signal, the applied SP realizes dynamic monitoring, and PDA uses enhanced the photothermal effect. The method is simple and easy to operate, providing a new technique for the rapid detection and in situ sterilization of foodborne pathogens. In addition, the micromotor is expected to realize the detection and sterilization of many other pathogens in a variety of complex food samples.

Wei Wu was responsible for the Conceptualization, Funding acquisition and the original draft; FeiFei Sun was responsible for method, data acquisition, Data curation, analysis, and manuscript writing; Mingru Yao and Hang Su was responsible for data acquisition and manuscript editing; Qingli Yang was responsible for manuscript revision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the National Key Research and Development Program of China [No. 2022YFF1102200], National Natural Science Foundation of China [No. U22A20541], the Breeding Plan of Shandong Provincial Qingchuang Research Team [No. 2019-135], the Qingdao Science and Technology Fund [No. 21-1-4-sf-6-nsh], High-value Intensive Processing and Development of Multi-level Coproduction Processing System of Rosa roxburghii [No. 6602421190] and the High-level Talent Start-up Fund from Qingdao Agricultural University [No. 663/1121045]. We thank Haier Biomedical for NIR technology assistant, and thank Dr. Jie Liu for magnetic drive motion assistant.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.snb.2023.133679.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Feifei Sun is a post-graduate student in Qingdao Agricultural University, China. She got her bachelor degree from Qingdao Agricultural University and was enroll as a post-graduate student in 2020. Her major is food safe and detection, and she focuses on the rapid detection assay establishment in foodborne pathogens.

Mingru Yao is a post-graduate student in Qingdao Agricultural University, China. She got her bachelor degree from Qingdao Agricultural University and was enroll as a post-graduate student in 2021. Her major is food safe and detection, and she focuses on the rapid detection assay establishment in foodborne pathogens.

Hang Su is a undergraduate student in Qingdao Agricultural University, China. She was enroll as a undergraduat student in 2020. Her major is food quality and safety, and she focuses on the rapid detection assay establishment in foodborne pathogens.

Qingli Yang is a Professor at the Qingdao Agricultural University, China. He got his doctor degree from Institute of Ocean, Chinese Academy of Sciences. His research interests are focused on the molecule mechanism of mycotoxin generation and rapid detection of mycotoxin in food. He has published over 100 academic articles.

Wei Wu is a Professor at the Qingdao Agricultural University, China. She got her doctor degree from Guangzhou Institutes of Biomedicine and Health, Chinese Academy of Sciences. After graduation, she joined Ocean University of China for her Post-doctor research. Her research interests are focused on new method or biosensors for food and environmental safety control, such as food-borne pathogens, toxins, heavy metals. She has published over 36 academic articles and 1 books.
